channel.title: Rabat Review of Books
channel.description: Essays, reviews, fiction, and poetry — from Rabat, for the world.

item.title: Four Short Poems
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Khribch
item.description:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2 Rabat · Paris · London
Subscribe About

Laila Amara’s recent collection Threshold (published last year by Casablanca House) has been praised for its compression and its refusal of sentimentality. Working in the mode of the short lyric—forms that span from two to ten lines—Amara channels the influence of both classical Arabic brevity and contemporary minimalism. These four poems, composed over the past six months, continue her investigation into how a single gesture, a overheard phrase, or the memory of light can contain an entire history. The poems are titled sequentially, suggesting a journey that is never fully named.

I.

My mother’s cousin left. We never learned which sea. Years later, a postcard arrived—no return address. It showed a lighthouse. My mother kept it next to the sugar. When I was seven, I assumed the lighthouse was keeping us safe from whatever my mother feared at night. I was not entirely wrong.

II.

There is a word in Darija for the space between saying yes and meaning it. My grandmother used it constantly. My generation has forgotten it. Now we are precise. Now we suffer clearly.

III.

The baker’s daughter married a man from the north. They moved to his village. Winter came early. She wrote that the snow fell upward— an error, surely, in translation. But I have never forgotten it. Upward snow. Toward what sky?

IV.

I keep a map of a city I have never seen. A friend’s grandmother gave it to her before she died. My friend gave it to me before she left. I touch the names like they are a form of prayer. Like they are a form of apology. Like they are the only home that cannot be taken.

Laila Amara was born in Marrakech and studied literature in Fez before earning her MFA from the University of Toronto. She currently teaches creative writing in Rabat and is at work on a second collection examining archives and belonging. Her poems have appeared in Asymptote, Guernica, and the 2025 Best African Poetry* anthology.*

item.title: Three Poems from The Bou Regreg
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Khribch
item.description:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2 Rabat · Paris · London
Subscribe About

The Bou Regreg is not merely a river—it is a keeper of contradictions. It flows through Rabat with the patience of a witness, having carried dynasties, trade, sorrow, and renewal. Yasmin Ighil, whose family traces roots to the Atlas foothills, approaches the river not as metaphor but as presence: a constant, tidal voice beneath the city’s surface. These three poems move from the river’s geological patience to its domestic intimacy, asking what it means to live beside something that is always leaving.

The Mouth

Where the river meets the grey Atlantic, the water forgets its name. The salt rises to meet it halfway—two forgettings becoming one. I have stood there in the glistening hour before dusk, watching the fresh turn brackish, turn indifferent. The herons do not hesitate. They wade as if they own the covenant between two elements learning to surrender.

Laundry Day

My grandmother knelt at the concrete lip where the river runs, her hands working cloth against the grooved stone as if she were speaking to someone downstream. The water was cold, even in August. She never once looked up at the medina walls or the palace, though they looked down at her. The soap—olive-colored, the size of her fist— turned to foam. The cloth darkened. Then slowly, like a secret, turned white again.

Current

At night the river knows something the city does not. It refuses to be still. Even in drought, even when the engineers speak of scarcity, it mutters its complaint in the darkness— a kind of prayer, a kind of refusal. My son dropped his blue marble in once, years ago. I told him it was traveling to the sea, to Spain, to places without names. He believed me. Sometimes I drive to the mouth and stand in the spray, looking, as if that marble might surface, blue and worn, demanding to be known again.

Yasmin Ighil was born in Fez and studied classical Arabic literature at the University of Rabat before moving to Casablanca to work in cultural preservation. Her first collection, “Stone Memory,” won the 2025 Tanger Prize. She lives near the Bou Regreg.

item.title: Ghazals for an Absent Friend
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Khribch
item.description:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2 Rabat · Paris · London
Subscribe About

The ghazal—that ancient Arabic form of connected, autonomous couplets—has long been a vessel for intimate address across unbridgeable distances. Karim Bendaoud, who spent a decade in Marrakech before relocating to Brussels, returns to this form to speak to a childhood friend from whom he has been geographically and circumstantially separated. What emerges is not so much nostalgia as a kind of radiant faithfulness: the beloved absence that paradoxically confirms connection. Bendaoud employs the classical ghazal’s capacity to collapse time and space while maintaining its formally broken form.

Ghazal: The Letter Never Sent

Dear friend, I am writing to you from a city where no one knows your name. The streets here smell of pine, not cedar. Still I find you everywhere.

The café where we first read that terrible translation of Rumi— it was destroyed in a flood. The floodwaters carried away your bookmark.

I did not save you. I could not. Each day I fail you differently. This too is a form of faithfulness, I think. Though you are not here to think it.

The olive seller on the avenue—he reminds me of your uncle. I buy from him and do not explain. What is there to say to a stranger?

You wrote that solitude is a river. I wrote back that you were wrong. Now I live beside one, and every day the water says your name.

The gardener here grows roses the color of old arguments. I do not ask her how. Some things should remain unexplained.

When winter comes, the city whites itself out like a photograph. All the faces I did not memorize become suddenly precious.

Dear friend, I am writing to you in pencil so I can erase it. Some forms of honesty require a way out.

Ghazal: Inheritance

You left me the blue cup. It arrived wrapped in newspaper from Fez. I have not drunk from it. This is how I know you are still alive.

My mother says this is foolishness. She drinks from every surface. But she did not know you when you were young and could be known.

The cup holds light differently than other cups. You knew this when you left it. You knew I would spend years learning what you already understood.

There is a spider living in the cupboard where I keep it. I think you would approve. You always said: let things find their own shelter.

Last month I dreamed you were in the market, selling figs. When I approached you turned away. In the dream I understood this was correct.

The blue cup sits in darkness. Above it, my reflection in the cupboard door. We are not the same person anymore. The cup remembers both of us.

You cannot write. I cannot ask. The spider has built a new room in the handle. This is how distance becomes domestic. This is how love persists.

I will not drink from the cup. But sometimes I hold it to the window. The light that passes through it lands on my palm like a small, impossible hand.

Karim Bendaoud teaches Arabic poetry at the Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam. He has published three collections in French and Arabic, and his work has appeared in Boundary 2, Asymptote, and various anthologies. He is currently working on a manuscript about diaspora and classical form.

item.title: Translations from the Arabic: Two Andalusian Fragments
item.dc:creator: Mehdi Khribch
item.description:
Spring 2026 · Vol. I · No. 2 Rabat · Paris · London
Subscribe About

The following two poems are original compositions by Amira Lahssen, conceived in the spirit and idiom of medieval Andalusian court poetry but written directly in English. They are not translations of historical texts, but rather original works that engage the aesthetic, philosophical, and formal preoccupations of the Andalusian tradition: the interplay of sensory precision and metaphysical longing, the mutability of power and beauty, the tension between the sacred and the erotic. Lahssen, whose research focuses on the crossroads of Arabic, Berber, and Iberian verse traditions, offers these as contemporary meditations on themes that haunted the poets of al-Andalus.

Fragment I: On the Courtyard Fountain

The fountain speaks in tongues. Each drop a syllable of cold devotion. The master who designed it has long dissolved into the earth he walked upon— his name erased, his tools distributed to his sons.

But the water remembers. It curves in the same arc he commanded, turns the air to gleaming, sends each prayer upward to the inattentive heavens.

The wife of the governor—she sits here at dusk listening not to the water’s voice but to the silence that surrounds it, vast as an ocean. The palace will burn. The empire will fracture. Yet this fountain will flow on, indifferent, perfect, beautiful as only the abandoned can be beautiful.

Fragment II: The Scribe’s Lament

I have copied seventeen verses today. The calligrapher’s hand is a bird that must obey the falconer, even as it flies.

The poem I inscribed was written by a man now dead, written of a beloved now dead, written in a language growing thin as parchment left too long in sun.

My fingers are stained with the colors of extinct empires. Gold leaf clings to my palm—the gold of ransomed kingdoms, the gold of merchants whose ships have sunk, the gold of saints who learned too late that holiness and hunger occupy the same body.

This afternoon a visitor asked if I believed in what I wrote. I dipped my brush in indigo— the blue of distance, the blue of eyes I will not see again— and did not answer.

What is belief but the refusal to stop? What is prayer but the continuation of the hand?

Amira Lahssen is a scholar and poet based in Tangier. She holds a doctorate in Medieval Arabic Literature from the University of Granada and has published work on the aesthetic exchanges between Christian and Islamic poetic traditions in medieval Iberia. Her essays appear in Journal of Medieval Islamic Poetry* and Mediterranean Review. This is her first publication of original poetry in English.*
